assumptions: The question concerns hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements.
The intragroup dividend has already been recognized as a receivable/payable and is therefore being assessed as a recognized intragroup balance rather than as a forecast transaction.
The receivable/payable is denominated in a currency that creates foreign exchange exposure between group entities with different functional currencies.
There is no evidence in the fact pattern that the balance is part of a qualifying net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: Foreign currency risk component of a recognized intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the resulting FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A specific risk component may be designated if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
conditions: the dividend receivable/payable is a recognized intragroup monetary item
the group entities involved have different functional currencies
the foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
the designated FX component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: Potentially yes: the hedge can target only the FX component, but only within this narrow intragroup monetary item exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements when the FX exposure is not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraphs 6.3.7, B6.3.8 and 6.4.1 require the FX component to be separately identifiable, reliably measurable, documented and effective.
IFRIC 16 provides a separate model for net investment hedges, so an ordinary intragroup receivable should first be assessed under the IFRS 9 intragroup monetary item exception.

approaches.label: General prohibition on hedging intragroup items in consolidated financial statements
approaches.reasoning: The general rule is that only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity can be hedged in consolidated financial statements. Intragroup items are excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: If the dividend balance does not meet a stated IFRS exception, hedge accounting is unavailable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidated financial statements to assets, liabilities, firm commitments or highly probable forecast transactions with parties external to the reporting entity.
IFRIC 16 addresses a separate narrow hedge model for net investments, reinforcing that intragroup hedge accounting is not generally available.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a specific exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions, but that model applies only before recognition and only when the FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. The stated fact pattern is a recognized receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: This does not justify hedge accounting for an already recognized intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits certain forecast intragroup FX exposures in consolidated financial statements if they are highly probable, foreign-currency denominated and affect consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraphs 6.3.3 and B6.3.5 restrict this exception to highly probable forecast transactions whose foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and should not be applied by analogy to other hedge relationships. An ordinary intragroup dividend receivable is not this hedge model on the facts provided.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on net investment hedge guidance unless the balance is substantively a qualifying net investment exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7 and 8 restrict the Interpretation to net investment hedges and state that it should not be applied by analogy to other types of hedge accounting.
Paragraphs 10 to 14 define the distinct hedged risk, hedged item and location of hedging instrument for net investment hedges.
Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment as a separate hedge type from fair value and cash flow hedges.
